February 5, 1919; 107 years ago (1919-02-05) in [Hollywood, Los Angeles](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles"), [California](/wiki/California "California"), United States (original; as United Artists Corporation) July 26, 2024; 19 months ago (2024-07-26) (relaunch; under the United Artists label)

* [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") * [Mary Pickford](/wiki/Mary_Pickford "Mary Pickford") * [Douglas Fairbanks](/wiki/Douglas_Fairbanks "Douglas Fairbanks") * [D. W. Griffith](/wiki/D._W._Griffith "D. W. Griffith")
[Culver City, California](/wiki/Culver_City,_California "Culver City, California") , United States

* [United Artists Records](/wiki/United_Artists_Records "United Artists Records") (1957–1978) * [United Artists Television](/wiki/United_Artists_Television "United Artists Television") (1958–1982) * United Artists Broadcasting (1968–1977) * United Artists Classics (1980–1984)

(**UA**) is an American [film production](/wiki/Film_production "Film production") and [distribution](/wiki/Film_distribution "Film distribution") company owned by [Amazon MGM Studios](/wiki/Amazon_MGM_Studios "Amazon MGM Studios"). In its original operating period, it was founded on February 5, 1919 by [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin"), [D. W. Griffith](/wiki/D._W._Griffith "D. W. Griffith"), [Mary Pickford](/wiki/Mary_Pickford "Mary Pickford") and [Douglas Fairbanks](/wiki/Douglas_Fairbanks "Douglas Fairbanks") as a venture premised on allowing actors to control their own financial and artistic interests rather than being dependent upon commercial studios.[[1]](#cite_note-LATimes-Woo-2011-09-29-1)

and [Lightworkers Media](/wiki/Lightworkers_Media "Lightworkers Media") and merged them to revive the television production unit of United Artists as [United Artists Media Group](/wiki/United_Artists_Media_Group "United Artists Media Group") (UAMG). MGM itself acquired UAMG on December 14, 2015, and folded it into [their own television division](/wiki/MGM_Television "MGM Television").[[3]](#cite_note-MGMTV-3)

for the *[Stargate Origins](/wiki/Stargate_Origins "Stargate Origins")* web series as part of [its *Stargate* franchise](/wiki/Stargate "Stargate"), but retired the name after 2019 and instead used its eponymous brand for subsequent releases.

**United Artists** (**UA**) is an American [film production](/wiki/Film_production "Film production") and [distribution](/wiki/Film_distribution "Film distribution") company owned by [Amazon MGM Studios](/wiki/Amazon_MGM_Studios "Amazon MGM Studios"). In its original operating period, it was founded on February 5, 1919 by [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin"), [D. W. Griffith](/wiki/D._W._Griffith "D. W. Griffith"), [Mary Pickford](/wiki/Mary_Pickford "Mary Pickford") and [Douglas Fairbanks](/wiki/Douglas_Fairbanks "Douglas Fairbanks") as a venture premised on allowing actors to control their own financial and artistic interests rather than being dependent upon commercial studios.[[1]](#cite_note-LATimes-Woo-2011-09-29-1)

After numerous ownership and structural changes and revamps, United Artists was acquired by [media conglomerate](/wiki/Media_conglomerate "Media conglomerate") [Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer") (MGM) in 1981 for a reported $350 million ($1.2 billion today).[[2]](#cite_note-2) On September 22, 2014, MGM acquired a controlling interest in **One Three Media** and [Lightworkers Media](/wiki/Lightworkers_Media "Lightworkers Media") and merged them to revive the television production unit of United Artists as [United Artists Media Group](/wiki/United_Artists_Media_Group "United Artists Media Group") (UAMG). MGM itself acquired UAMG on December 14, 2015, and folded it into [their own television division](/wiki/MGM_Television "MGM Television").[[3]](#cite_note-MGMTV-3)

MGM briefly revived the United Artists brand as **United Artists Digital Studios** for the *[Stargate Origins](/wiki/Stargate_Origins "Stargate Origins")* web series as part of [its *Stargate* franchise](/wiki/Stargate "Stargate"), but retired the name after 2019 and instead used its eponymous brand for subsequent releases.

A local joint distribution venture between MGM and [Annapurna Pictures](/wiki/Annapurna_Pictures "Annapurna Pictures") launched on October 31, 2017[[4]](#cite_note-MFound-4) was rebranded as [United Artists Releasing](/wiki/United_Artists_Releasing "United Artists Releasing") on February 5, 2019, in honor of its 100th anniversary.[[5]](#cite_note-Rebrand-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Rebrand1-6) However, [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)"), MGM's now-parent company, folded UAR into MGM on March 4, 2023, citing "newfound theatrical release opportunities" following the box-office opening success of *[Creed III](/wiki/Creed_III "Creed III")*.[[7]](#cite_note-Creed-7)

On July 26, 2024, [Amazon MGM Studios](/wiki/Amazon_MGM_Studios "Amazon MGM Studios") announced the company's revival, entering a multi-year first look deal with film producer [Scott Stuber](/wiki/Scott_Stuber "Scott Stuber"), who will also be involved with all releases under the freshly revived production company.

## History

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/United_Artists_1919.svg/250px-United_Artists_1919.svg.png)](/wiki/File:United_Artists_1919.svg)

United Artists' first logo, used until the company's sale to Transamerica in 1967

### Early years

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Fairbanks_-_Pickford_-_Chaplin_-_Griffith.jpg/250px-Fairbanks_-_Pickford_-_Chaplin_-_Griffith.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fairbanks_-_Pickford_-_Chaplin_-_Griffith.jpg)

[Douglas Fairbanks](/wiki/Douglas_Fairbanks "Douglas Fairbanks"), [Mary Pickford](/wiki/Mary_Pickford "Mary Pickford"), [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin"), and [D. W. Griffith](/wiki/D._W._Griffith "D. W. Griffith") in 1919

In 1918, [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") could not get his parent company [First National Pictures](/wiki/First_National_Pictures "First National Pictures") to increase his production budget despite being one of their top producers. [Mary Pickford](/wiki/Mary_Pickford "Mary Pickford") and [Douglas Fairbanks](/wiki/Douglas_Fairbanks "Douglas Fairbanks") had their own contracts, with First National and [Famous Players–Lasky](/wiki/Famous_Players%E2%80%93Lasky "Famous Players–Lasky") respectively, but these were due to run out with no clear offers forthcoming. Sydney Chaplin, brother and business manager for Charlie, deduced something was going wrong, and contacted Pickford and Fairbanks. Together they hired a private detective, who discovered a plan to merge all production companies and to lock in "exhibition companies" to a series of five-year contracts.[[8]](#cite_note-uacbs-8)

Chaplin, Pickford, Fairbanks, and [D. W. Griffith](/wiki/D._W._Griffith "D. W. Griffith") incorporated United Artists as a joint venture company on February 5, 1919. Each held a 25% stake in the [preferred shares](/wiki/Preferred_stock "Preferred stock") and a 20% stake in the [common shares](/wiki/Common_stock "Common stock") of the joint venture, with the remaining 20% of common shares held by lawyer and advisor [William Gibbs McAdoo](/wiki/William_Gibbs_McAdoo "William Gibbs McAdoo").[[9]](#cite_note-siklos-9) The idea for the venture originated with Fairbanks, Chaplin, Pickford and cowboy star [William S. Hart](/wiki/William_S._Hart "William S. Hart") a year earlier. Already Hollywood veterans, the four stars talked of forming their own company to better control their own work.

They were spurred on by established Hollywood producers and distributors who were tightening their control over actor salaries and creative decisions, a process that evolved into the [studio system](/wiki/Studio_system "Studio system"). With the addition of Griffith, planning began, but Hart bowed out before anything was formalized. When he heard about their scheme, [Richard A. Rowland](/wiki/Richard_A._Rowland "Richard A. Rowland"), head of [Metro Pictures](/wiki/Metro_Pictures "Metro Pictures"), apparently said, "The inmates are taking over the asylum."[[10]](#cite_note-10) The four partners, with advice from McAdoo (son-in-law and former [Treasury Secretary](/wiki/U.S._Secretary_of_the_Treasury "U.S. Secretary of the Treasury") of then-President [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson")), formed their distribution company. [Hiram Abrams](/wiki/Hiram_Abrams "Hiram Abrams") was its first managing director, and the company established its headquarters at 729 Seventh Avenue in New York City.[[8]](#cite_note-uacbs-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/United_Artists_Stockholders.jpg/250px-United_Artists_Stockholders.jpg)](/wiki/File:United_Artists_Stockholders.jpg)

List of UA stockholders in 1920

The original terms called for each star to produce five pictures a year. By the time the company was operational in 1921, feature films were becoming more expensive and polished, and running times had settled at around ninety minutes (eight reels). The original goal was thus abandoned.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/United_Artists_contract_signature_1919.jpg/250px-United_Artists_contract_signature_1919.jpg)](/wiki/File:United_Artists_contract_signature_1919.jpg)

[Griffith](/wiki/D._W._Griffith "D. W. Griffith"), [Pickford](/wiki/Mary_Pickford "Mary Pickford"), [Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") (seated), and [Fairbanks](/wiki/Douglas_Fairbanks "Douglas Fairbanks") at the signing of the contract establishing the United Artists motion-picture studio in 1919.  
Lawyers Albert Banzhaf (left)  
and Dennis F. O'Brien (right)  
stand in the background.

UA's first production, *[His Majesty, the American](/wiki/His_Majesty,_the_American "His Majesty, the American")*, written by and starring Fairbanks, was a success. Funding for movies was limited. Without selling stock to the public like other studios, all United had for finance was weekly prepayment installments from theater owners for upcoming movies. As a result, production was slow, and the company distributed an average of only five films a year in its first five years.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*]

By 1924, Griffith had dropped out, and the company was facing a crisis.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Veteran producer [Joseph Schenck](/wiki/Joseph_Schenck "Joseph Schenck") was hired as president.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11) He had produced pictures for a decade,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and brought commitments for films starring his wife, [Norma Talmadge](/wiki/Norma_Talmadge "Norma Talmadge"),[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11) his sister-in-law, [Constance Talmadge](/wiki/Constance_Talmadge "Constance Talmadge"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and his brother-in-law, [Buster Keaton](/wiki/Buster_Keaton "Buster Keaton").[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11) Contracts were signed with independent producers, including [Samuel Goldwyn](/wiki/Samuel_Goldwyn "Samuel Goldwyn"), and [Howard Hughes](/wiki/Howard_Hughes "Howard Hughes").[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11) In 1933, Schenck organized a new company with [Darryl F. Zanuck](/wiki/Darryl_F._Zanuck "Darryl F. Zanuck"), called [Twentieth Century Pictures](/wiki/Twentieth_Century_Pictures "Twentieth Century Pictures"), which soon provided four pictures a year, forming half of UA's schedule.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11)

Schenck formed a separate partnership with Pickford and Chaplin to buy and build theaters under the United Artists name. They began international operations, first in Canada, and then in Mexico. By the end of the 1930s, United Artists was represented in over 40 countries.

When he was denied an ownership share in 1935, Schenck resigned. He set up [20th Century Pictures](/wiki/Twentieth_Century_Pictures "Twentieth Century Pictures")' merger with [Fox Film Corporation](/wiki/Fox_Film "Fox Film") to form [20th Century-Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Studios "20th Century Studios").[[12]](#cite_note-12) [Al Lichtman](/wiki/Al_Lichtman "Al Lichtman") succeeded Schenck as company president. Other independent producers distributed through United Artists in the 1930s including [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney"), [Alexander Korda](/wiki/Alexander_Korda "Alexander Korda"), [Hal Roach](/wiki/Hal_Roach "Hal Roach"), [David O. Selznick](/wiki/David_O._Selznick "David O. Selznick"), and [Walter Wanger](/wiki/Walter_Wanger "Walter Wanger").[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11) As the years passed, and the dynamics of the business changed, these "producing partners" drifted away. [Samuel Goldwyn Productions](/wiki/Samuel_Goldwyn_Productions "Samuel Goldwyn Productions") and [Walt Disney Productions](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Productions "Walt Disney Productions") went to [RKO Radio Pictures](/wiki/RKO_Radio_Pictures "RKO Radio Pictures") and Wanger to [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures").

In the late 1930s, UA turned a profit. Goldwyn was providing most of the output for distribution. He sued United several times for disputed compensation leading him to leave. MGM's 1939 hit *[Gone with the Wind](/wiki/Gone_with_the_Wind_(film) "Gone with the Wind (film)")* was supposed to be a UA release except that Selznick wanted [Clark Gable](/wiki/Clark_Gable "Clark Gable"), who was under contract to MGM, to play [Rhett Butler](/wiki/Rhett_Butler "Rhett Butler"). Also that year, Fairbanks died.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11)

UA became embroiled in lawsuits with Selznick over his distribution of some films through RKO. Selznick considered UA's operation sloppy, and left to start his own distribution arm.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11)

In the 1940s, United Artists was losing money because of poorly received pictures.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Cinema attendance continued to decline as television became more popular.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11) The company sold its Mexican releasing division to Crédito Cinematográfico Mexicano, a local company.

### Society of Independent Motion Picture Producers (1940s and 1950s)

In 1941, Pickford, Chaplin, Disney, [Orson Welles](/wiki/Orson_Welles "Orson Welles"), Goldwyn, Selznick, Alexander Korda, and Wanger—many of whom were members of United Artists—formed the Society of Independent Motion Picture Producers (SIMPP). Later members included [Hunt Stromberg](/wiki/Hunt_Stromberg "Hunt Stromberg"), [William Cagney](/wiki/William_Cagney "William Cagney"), [Sol Lesser](/wiki/Sol_Lesser "Sol Lesser"), and [Hal Roach](/wiki/Hal_Roach "Hal Roach").

The Society aimed to advance the interests of independent producers in an industry controlled by the studio system. SIMPP fought to end what were considered to be anti-competitive practices by the seven major film studios—Loew's (MGM), [Columbia Pictures](/wiki/Columbia_Pictures "Columbia Pictures"), [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures"), [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures"), RKO Radio Pictures, 20th Century-Fox, and [Warner Bros./First National](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures")—that controlled the production, distribution, and exhibition of motion pictures.

In 1942, SIMPP filed an antitrust suit against Paramount's [United Detroit Theatres](/wiki/United_Paramount_Theaters "United Paramount Theaters"). The complaint accused Paramount of conspiracy to control first-and subsequent-run theaters in Detroit. This was the first antitrust suit brought by producers against exhibitors that alleged monopoly and restraint of trade. In 1948, the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court "United States Supreme Court") [Paramount Decision](/wiki/Paramount_Decision "Paramount Decision") ordered the major Hollywood [movie studios](/wiki/Movie_studio "Movie studio") to sell their theater chains and to end certain anti-competitive practices. This court ruling ended the studio system.

By 1958, SIMPP had achieved many of the goals that led to its creation, and the group ceased operations.

### Krim and Benjamin

Needing a turnaround, Pickford and Chaplin hired [Paul V. McNutt](/wiki/Paul_V._McNutt "Paul V. McNutt") in 1950,[[13]](#cite_note-UA40-13) a former governor of Indiana, as chairman and [Frank L. McNamee](/wiki/Frank_L._McNamee "Frank L. McNamee") as president. McNutt did not have the skill to solve UA's financial problems and the pair was replaced after only a few months.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11)

On February 15, 1951, lawyers-turned-producers [Arthur B. Krim](/wiki/Arthur_B._Krim "Arthur B. Krim") (of [Eagle-Lion Films](/wiki/Eagle-Lion_Films "Eagle-Lion Films")), [Robert Benjamin](/wiki/Robert_Benjamin "Robert Benjamin") and Matty Fox[[13]](#cite_note-UA40-13) approached Pickford and Chaplin with an idea: let them take over United Artists for ten years. If UA was profitable in one of the next three years, they would have the option to acquire half the company by the end of the ten years and take full control.[[13]](#cite_note-UA40-13) 20th Century-Fox president [Spyros Skouras](/wiki/Spyros_Skouras "Spyros Skouras") extended United Artists a $3 million loan through Krim and Benjamin's efforts.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11)[[14]](#cite_note-vt0-14)

In taking over UA, Krim and Benjamin created the first studio without an actual "studio". Primarily acting as bankers, they offered money to independent producers. UA leased space at the Pickford/Fairbanks Studio but did not own a studio lot. Thus UA did not have the overhead, the maintenance, or the expensive production staff at other studios.

Among their first clients were [Sam Spiegel](/wiki/Sam_Spiegel "Sam Spiegel") and [John Huston](/wiki/John_Huston "John Huston"), whose Horizon Productions gave UA one major hit, *[The African Queen](/wiki/The_African_Queen_(film) "The African Queen (film)")* (1951) and a substantial success, *[Moulin Rouge](/wiki/Moulin_Rouge_(1952_film) "Moulin Rouge (1952 film)")* (1952). Besides *The African Queen* UA also had success with *[High Noon](/wiki/High_Noon "High Noon")* in their first year, earning a profit of $313,000 compared to a loss of $871,000 the previous year.[[13]](#cite_note-UA40-13)[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11) Other clients followed, among them [Stanley Kramer](/wiki/Stanley_Kramer "Stanley Kramer"), [Otto Preminger](/wiki/Otto_Preminger "Otto Preminger"), [Hecht-Hill-Lancaster Productions](/wiki/Hecht-Hill-Lancaster_Productions "Hecht-Hill-Lancaster Productions"), and actors newly freed from studio contracts and seeking to produce or direct their own films.

With the instability in the film industry due to theater divestment, the business was considered risky. In 1955, movie attendance reached its lowest level since 1923. Chaplin sold his 25% share during this crisis to Krim and Benjamin for $1.1 million, followed a year later by Pickford who sold her share for $3 million.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11)

In the late 1950s, United Artists produced two modest films that became financial and critical successes for the company. The company made *[Marty](/wiki/Marty_(film) "Marty (film)")* which won 1955's Palme d'Or and Best Picture in the Oscars, and *[12 Angry Men](/wiki/12_Angry_Men_(1957_film) "12 Angry Men (1957 film)")* (1957), which according to Krim before home video, was being seen on television 24 hours a day, 365 days a year some place in the world.[[14]](#cite_note-vt0-14) By 1958, UA was making annual profits of $3 million a year.[[13]](#cite_note-UA40-13)

### Public company

United Artists went public in 1957 with a $17 million stock and [debenture](/wiki/Debenture "Debenture") offering. The company was averaging 50 films a year.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11) In 1958, UA acquired [Ilya Lopert](/wiki/Ilya_Lopert "Ilya Lopert")'s [Lopert Pictures Corporation](/wiki/Lopert_Pictures_Corporation "Lopert Pictures Corporation"), which released foreign films that attracted criticism or was censoed elsewhere.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 1957, UA created [United Artists Records](/wiki/United_Artists_Records "United Artists Records") Corporation and United Artists Music Corporation after an unsuccessful attempt to buy a record company.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In 1968, UA Records merged with [Liberty Records](/wiki/Liberty_Records "Liberty Records"), along with its many subsidiary labels such as [Imperial Records](/wiki/Imperial_Records "Imperial Records") and [Dolton Records](/wiki/Dolton_Records "Dolton Records"). In 1972, the group was consolidated into one entity as United Artists Records and in 1979, [EMI](/wiki/EMI "EMI") acquired the division which included [Blue Note Records](/wiki/Blue_Note_Records "Blue Note Records").[[17]](#cite_note-EMI-17)

In 1959, after failing to sell several pilots, United Artists offered its first ever television series, *[The Troubleshooters](/wiki/The_Troubleshooters_(1959_TV_series) "The Troubleshooters (1959 TV series)")*,[[18]](#cite_note-18) and later released its first sitcom, *[The Dennis O'Keefe Show](/wiki/The_Dennis_O%27Keefe_Show "The Dennis O'Keefe Show")*.

In the 1960s, mainstream studios fell into decline and some were acquired or diversified. UA prospered while winning 11 Academy Awards, including five for Best Picture,[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11) adding relationships with the [Mirisch brothers](/wiki/Mirisch_Company "Mirisch Company"), [Billy Wilder](/wiki/Billy_Wilder "Billy Wilder"), [Joseph E. Levine](/wiki/Joseph_E._Levine "Joseph E. Levine") and others. In 1961, United Artists released *[West Side Story](/wiki/West_Side_Story_(1961_film) "West Side Story (1961 film)")*, which won ten [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") (including [Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture "Academy Award for Best Picture")).

In 1960, UA purchased [Ziv Television Programs](/wiki/Ziv_Television_Programs "Ziv Television Programs"). [UA's television division](/wiki/United_Artists_Television "United Artists Television") was responsible for shows such as *[Gilligan's Island](/wiki/Gilligan%27s_Island "Gilligan's Island"),* *[The Fugitive](/wiki/The_Fugitive_(1963_TV_series) "The Fugitive (1963 TV series)")*, *[Outer Limits](/wiki/The_Outer_Limits_(1963_TV_series) "The Outer Limits (1963 TV series)")*, and *[The Patty Duke Show](/wiki/The_Patty_Duke_Show "The Patty Duke Show")*. The television unit had begun to build up a profitable rental library, including [Associated Artists Productions](/wiki/Associated_Artists_Productions "Associated Artists Productions"),[[19]](#cite_note-19) owners of [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures")' pre-1950[[20]](#cite_note-Schickel-20)[[a]](#cite_note-21) features, shorts and cartoons and 231 *[Popeye](/wiki/Popeye_the_Sailor_(animated_cartoons) "Popeye the Sailor (animated cartoons)")* cartoon shorts purchased from [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") in 1958, becoming [United Artists Associated](/wiki/United_Artists_Associated "United Artists Associated"), its distribution division.

In 1963, UA released two Stanley Kramer films, *[It's A Mad, Mad, Mad, Mad World](/wiki/It%27s_A_Mad,_Mad,_Mad,_Mad_World "It's A Mad, Mad, Mad, Mad World")* and *[A Child Is Waiting](/wiki/A_Child_Is_Waiting "A Child Is Waiting")*. In 1964, UA introduced U.S. film audiences to [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") by releasing *[A Hard Day's Night](/wiki/A_Hard_Day%27s_Night_(film) "A Hard Day's Night (film)")* (1964) and *[Help!](/wiki/Help!_(film) "Help! (film)")* (1965).

At the same time, it backed two expatriate North Americans in Britain, who had acquired screen rights to [Ian Fleming](/wiki/Ian_Fleming "Ian Fleming")'s [James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond "James Bond") novels. For $1 million, UA backed [Harry Saltzman](/wiki/Harry_Saltzman "Harry Saltzman") and [Albert Broccoli](/wiki/Albert_Broccoli "Albert Broccoli")'s *[Dr. No](/wiki/Dr._No_(film) "Dr. No (film)")* in 1963 and launched the [James Bond franchise](/wiki/James_Bond_(film_series) "James Bond (film series)").[[21]](#cite_note-VF-22) The franchise outlived UA's time as a major studio, continuing half a century later. Other successful projects backed in this period included the *[Pink Panther](/wiki/The_Pink_Panther_(film_series) "The Pink Panther (film series)")* series, which began in 1964, and [Spaghetti Westerns](/wiki/Spaghetti_Western "Spaghetti Western"), which made a star of [Clint Eastwood](/wiki/Clint_Eastwood "Clint Eastwood") in the films of *[A Fistful of Dollars](/wiki/A_Fistful_of_Dollars "A Fistful of Dollars")*, *[For a Few Dollars More](/wiki/For_a_Few_Dollars_More "For a Few Dollars More")* and *[The Good, The Bad and The Ugly](/wiki/The_Good,_The_Bad_and_The_Ugly "The Good, The Bad and The Ugly")*.

In 1964, the French subsidiary, Les Productions Artistes Associés, released its first production *[That Man from Rio](/wiki/That_Man_from_Rio "That Man from Rio")*.

In 1965, UA released the anticipated George Stevens' production of *[The Greatest Story Ever Told](/wiki/The_Greatest_Story_Ever_Told "The Greatest Story Ever Told")* and was at the time, the most expensive film which was budgeted at $20 million. Max Von Sydow, in the role of Jesus Christ, led an all-star cast which included Charlton Heston, Roddy McDowell, Martin Landau, Dorothy McGuire, Sal Mineo, Ina Balin, Joanna Dunham, David McCallum, Nehemiah Persoff, Donald Pleasence, José Ferrer and Ed Wynn. The film did not make back its budget and was released to mixed critical reception. However, it has since been acclaimed as a classic by audiences around the world for being admirably inspired in its attempt to be faithful to the four books of the New Testament in the Holy Bible as well as the book of the same name by [Fulton Oursler](/wiki/Fulton_Oursler "Fulton Oursler") and the [radio program](/wiki/The_Greatest_Story_Ever_Told_(radio_program) "The Greatest Story Ever Told (radio program)") which ran from 1947 to 1956. *The Greatest Story Ever Told* received five Academy Award nominations in 1965 and was also listed among the "Top 10 Films of the Year" by the [National Board of Review](/wiki/National_Board_of_Review "National Board of Review").

### Transamerica subsidiary

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/United_Artists_logo_1975.svg/250px-United_Artists_logo_1975.svg.png)](/wiki/File:United_Artists_logo_1975.svg)

United Artists' second logo, used during the company's sale to Transamerica from 1967 until 1982

On the basis of its film and television hits, in 1967, [Transamerica Corporation](/wiki/Transamerica_Corporation "Transamerica Corporation") purchased 98% of UA's stock. Transamerica selected [David](/wiki/David_V._Picker "David V. Picker") and [Arnold Picker](/wiki/Arnold_Picker "Arnold Picker") to lead its studio.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11) UA debuted a new logo incorporating the parent company's striped T emblem and the tagline "Entertainment from Transamerica Corporation". This wording was later shortened to "A Transamerica Company". The following year, in 1968, United Artists Associated was reincorporated as United Artists Television Distribution. In 1970, UA lost $35 million, and the Pickers were pushed aside for the return of Krim and Benjamin.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11)

UA released another Best Picture Oscar winner in 1967, *[In the Heat of the Night](/wiki/In_the_Heat_of_the_Night_(film) "In the Heat of the Night (film)")* and a nominee for Best Picture, *[The Graduate](/wiki/The_Graduate "The Graduate")*, an [Embassy](/wiki/Embassy_Pictures "Embassy Pictures") production that UA distributed overseas.

Other successful pictures included the [1971 screen version](/wiki/Fiddler_on_the_Roof_(film) "Fiddler on the Roof (film)") of *[Fiddler on the Roof](/wiki/Fiddler_on_the_Roof "Fiddler on the Roof")*. However, [the 1972 film version of *Man of La Mancha*](/wiki/Man_of_La_Mancha_(film) "Man of La Mancha (film)") was a failure. New talent was encouraged, including [Woody Allen](/wiki/Woody_Allen "Woody Allen"), [Robert Altman](/wiki/Robert_Altman "Robert Altman"), [Sylvester Stallone](/wiki/Sylvester_Stallone "Sylvester Stallone"), [Saul Zaentz](/wiki/Saul_Zaentz "Saul Zaentz"), [Miloš Forman](/wiki/Milo%C5%A1_Forman "Miloš Forman"), and [Brian De Palma](/wiki/Brian_De_Palma "Brian De Palma"). With UA being the distributor for Woody Allen's *[Bananas](/wiki/Bananas_(film) "Bananas (film)")* (1971),[[22]](#cite_note-23) it started as the distributor for a series of Woody Allen films. With the James Bond, Pink Panther, and Woody Allen films, UA had a series of films based on well known characters in the 1970s.

In 1973, United Artists took over the sales and distribution of [MGM](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer")'s films in [Anglo-America](/wiki/Anglo-America "Anglo-America"). [Cinema International Corporation](/wiki/Cinema_International_Corporation "Cinema International Corporation") assumed international distribution rights for MGM's films and carried on to United International Pictures (made from CIC and UA's International assets being owned by partner MGM) in the 1980s. As part of the deal, UA acquired MGM's music publishing operation, Robbins, Feist, Miller.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

In 1975, Harry Saltzman sold UA his 50% stake in [Danjaq](/wiki/Danjaq "Danjaq"), the holding-company for the Bond films.

UA agreed to release *[One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest](/wiki/One_Flew_Over_the_Cuckoo%27s_Nest_(film) "One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest (film)")* in 1975, after many other studios turned it down. Producer [Michael Douglas](/wiki/Michael_Douglas "Michael Douglas") later said "United Artists, respectfully, was like your last choice in those days but they made us a decent deal".[[24]](#cite_note-jurgensen20250708-25) The film won the Best Picture Academy Award and was UA's highest-grossing film, with a gross of $163 million.[[25]](#cite_note-26) UA followed with the next two years' Best Picture Oscar winners, *[Rocky](/wiki/Rocky "Rocky")* and *[Annie Hall](/wiki/Annie_Hall "Annie Hall")*,[[24]](#cite_note-jurgensen20250708-25) becoming the first studio to win the award for three years running and also to become the studio with the most Best Picture winners at that time, with 11.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11)[[26]](#cite_note-27)

However, Transamerica was not pleased with UA's releases such as *[Midnight Cowboy](/wiki/Midnight_Cowboy "Midnight Cowboy")* and *[Last Tango in Paris](/wiki/Last_Tango_in_Paris "Last Tango in Paris")* that were [rated X](/wiki/X_rating "X rating") by the [Motion Picture Association of America](/wiki/MPAA "MPAA"). In these instances, Transamerica demanded the byline "A Transamerica Company" be removed on the prints and in all advertising. At one point, the parent company expressed its desire to phase out the UA name and replace it with Transamerica Films. Krim tried to convince Transamerica to spin off United Artists, but he and Transamerica's chairman could not come to an agreement.[[27]](#cite_note-Medavoy-28) Finally in 1978, following a dispute with Transamerica chief [John R. Beckett](/wiki/John_R._Beckett "John R. Beckett")[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11) over administrative expenses,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] UA's top executives, including chairman Krim, president [Eric Pleskow](/wiki/Eric_Pleskow "Eric Pleskow"), Benjamin and other key officers walked out. Within days they announced the formation of [Orion Pictures](/wiki/Orion_Pictures "Orion Pictures"),[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11) with backing from [Warner Bros](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures"). The departures concerned several Hollywood figures enough that they took out an ad in a trade paper warning Transamerica that it had made a fatal mistake in letting them go.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Later that year, it entered into a partnership with [Lorimar Productions](/wiki/Lorimar_Television "Lorimar Television"), whereas United Artists would distribute Lorimar's feature films theatrically, while Lorimar was planning to produce television series and miniseries adaptations from UA's feature film library.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

Transamerica inserted Andy Albeck as UA's president. United had its most successful year with four hits in 1979: *[Rocky II](/wiki/Rocky_II "Rocky II")*, *[Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan_(1979_film) "Manhattan (1979 film)")*, *[Moonraker](/wiki/Moonraker_(film) "Moonraker (film)")*, and *[The Black Stallion](/wiki/The_Black_Stallion_(film) "The Black Stallion (film)")*.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11)

The new leadership agreed to back *[Heaven's Gate](/wiki/Heaven%27s_Gate_(film) "Heaven's Gate (film)")*, a project of director [Michael Cimino](/wiki/Michael_Cimino "Michael Cimino"), which vastly overran its budget and cost $44 million. This led to the resignation of Albeck, who was replaced by Norbert Auerbach.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11) United Artists recorded a major loss for the year due almost entirely to the box-office failure of *Heaven's Gate*.[[29]](#cite_note-30) It destroyed UA's reputation with Transamerica and the greater Hollywood community. However, it may have saved the United Artists name; UA's final head before the sale, [Steven Bach](/wiki/Steven_Bach "Steven Bach"), wrote in his book *Final Cut* that there had been talk about renaming United Artists to Transamerica Pictures.

In 1980, Transamerica decided to exit the film making business, and put United Artists on the market. [Kirk Kerkorian](/wiki/Kirk_Kerkorian "Kirk Kerkorian")'s Tracinda Corp. purchased the company in 1981.[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-Fleming-32) Tracinda also owned Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer.[[32]](#cite_note-nyt-33)

#### United Artists Classics

In 1981, United Artists Classics, which formerly re-released library titles, was turned into a first-run [art film](/wiki/Art_film "Art film") distributor by Nathaniel T. Kwit, Jr. [Tom Bernard](/wiki/Tom_Bernard "Tom Bernard") was hired as the division director, as well as handling theatrical sales, and [Ira Deutchman](/wiki/Ira_Deutchman "Ira Deutchman")[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35) was hired as head of marketing. Later the division added [Michael Barker](/wiki/Michael_Barker_(film_distributor) "Michael Barker (film distributor)") and [Donna Gigliotti](/wiki/Donna_Gigliotti "Donna Gigliotti"). Deutchman left to form [Cinecom](/wiki/Cinecom "Cinecom"), and Barker and Bernard formed [Orion Classics](/wiki/Orion_Classics "Orion Classics") and [Sony Pictures Classics](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Classics "Sony Pictures Classics"). The label mostly released foreign and independent films such as *[Cutter's Way](/wiki/Cutter%27s_Way "Cutter's Way")*, *[Ticket to Heaven](/wiki/Ticket_to_Heaven "Ticket to Heaven")* and *[The Grey Fox](/wiki/The_Grey_Fox "The Grey Fox")*, and occasional first-run reissues from the UA library, such as director's cuts of *[Head Over Heels](/wiki/Head_Over_Heels_(1979_film) "Head Over Heels (1979 film)")*. When Barker and Bernard left to form Orion Classics, the label was briefly rechristened in 1984 as MGM/UA Classics before it ceased operating in the late 1980s.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

### MGM/UA Entertainment Company

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/United_Artists_logo_1982.svg/250px-United_Artists_logo_1982.svg.png)](/wiki/File:United_Artists_logo_1982.svg)

Logo used from 1982 to 1987

The merged studios became known as the MGM/UA Entertainment Company, and in 1982 began launching new subsidiaries: the [MGM/UA Home Entertainment Group](/wiki/MGM_Home_Entertainment "MGM Home Entertainment"), MGM/UA Classics and [MGM/UA Television Group](/wiki/MGM_Television "MGM Television"). Kerkorian also bid for the remaining, outstanding public stock, but dropped his bid, facing lawsuits and vocal opposition.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11)

In 1981, [Fred Silverman](/wiki/Fred_Silverman "Fred Silverman") and George Reeves via InterMedia Entertainment struck a deal with the studio to produce films and television shows.[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)

After the purchase, [David Begelman](/wiki/David_Begelman "David Begelman")'s duties were transferred from MGM to MGM/UA. Under Begelman, MGM/UA produced unsuccessful films and he was fired in July 1982. Of the 11 films he put into production, by the time of his termination only *[Poltergeist](/wiki/Poltergeist_(film_series) "Poltergeist (film series)")* proved to be a hit.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

As part of the consolidation, in 1983, MGM closed United Artists' long time headquarters at 729 Seventh Avenue in New York City.[[39]](#cite_note-40) MGM/UA sold the former UA music publishing division to [CBS Songs](/wiki/SBK_Records "SBK Records") in 1983.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

*[WarGames](/wiki/WarGames "WarGames")* and *[Octopussy](/wiki/Octopussy "Octopussy")* made substantial profits for the new MGM/UA in 1983, but were not sufficient for Kerkorian. It did not help tha*t WarGames* was briefly caught in a legal dispute with [EMI Films](/wiki/EMI_Films "EMI Films") concerning the film's funding and international rights.[[41]](#cite_note-42) A 1985-restructuring led to independent MGM and UA production units with the combined studio leaders each placed in charge of a single unit. Speculation from analysts was that one of the studios, most likely UA, would be sold to fund the other's (MGM) stock buy-back to take that studio private. However, soon afterwards, one unit's chief was fired and the remaining executive, [Alan Ladd, Jr.](/wiki/Alan_Ladd,_Jr. "Alan Ladd, Jr."), took charge of both.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11)

#### Turner

On August 7, 1985, [Ted Turner](/wiki/Ted_Turner "Ted Turner") announced that his [Turner Broadcasting System](/wiki/Turner_Broadcasting_System "Turner Broadcasting System") would buy MGM/UA. As film licensing to television became more complicated, Turner saw the value of acquiring MGM's film library for his superstation [WTBS](/wiki/TBS_(TV_network) "TBS (TV network)").[[42]](#cite_note-Prince-43) Under the terms of the deal, Turner would immediately sell United Artists back to Kerkorian.[[32]](#cite_note-nyt-33)

In anticipation, Kerkorian installed film producer [Jerry Weintraub](/wiki/Jerry_Weintraub "Jerry Weintraub") as the chairman and chief executive of United Artists Corporation in November 1985; former [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") executive [Anthony Thomopoulos](/wiki/Anthony_Thomopoulos "Anthony Thomopoulos") was recruited as UA's president.[[43]](#cite_note-44)[[44]](#cite_note-45) Weintraub's tenure at UA was brief; he left the studio in April 1986, replaced by former [Lorimar](/wiki/Lorimar_Television "Lorimar Television") executive [Lee Rich](/wiki/Lee_Rich "Lee Rich").[[45]](#cite_note-46) In anticipation, during the split, SLM Production Group moved its distribution deal to United Artists, after having left MGM/UA temporarily for 20th Century Fox.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

On March 25, 1986, Turner finalized his acquisition of MGM/UA in a cash-stock deal for $1.5 billion and renamed it MGM Entertainment Co.[[42]](#cite_note-Prince-43)[[47]](#cite_note-Bart-48)[[48]](#cite_note-Turner-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52) Kerkorian then repurchased most of United Artists' assets for roughly $480 million.[[47]](#cite_note-Bart-48)[[48]](#cite_note-Turner-49) As a result of this transaction, the original United Artists ceased to exist. Kerkorian, for all intents and purposes, created an entirely new company implementing the inherited assets; thus, the present day UA is not the legal successor to the original incarnation, though it shares similar assets.[[52]](#cite_note-Balio-53) United Artists announced plans to launch its new headquarters on [Beverly Hills](/wiki/Beverly_Hills,_California "Beverly Hills, California"), which was set to take effect on November 1, 1985, shortly before the Turner deal was finalized.[[53]](#cite_note-54) On April 23, 1986, United Artists and [Hoyts](/wiki/Hoyts "Hoyts"), the Australian cinema chain and distribution company, signed a three-picture deal to co-produce films.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

### MGM/UA Communications Company

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/United_Artists_1987.svg/250px-United_Artists_1987.svg.png)](/wiki/File:United_Artists_1987.svg)

Logo used from 1987 to 1994

Due to financial community concerns over his debt load, Ted Turner was forced to sell MGM's production and distribution assets to United Artists for $300 million on August 26, 1986.[[47]](#cite_note-Bart-48)[[48]](#cite_note-Turner-49)[[55]](#cite_note-Fabrikant-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57) The MGM lot and lab facilities were sold to [Lorimar-Telepictures](/wiki/Lorimar-Telepictures "Lorimar-Telepictures").[[55]](#cite_note-Fabrikant-56) Turner kept the pre-May 1986 MGM film and television library, along with the [Associated Artists Productions](/wiki/Associated_Artists_Productions "Associated Artists Productions") library, and the RKO Pictures films that United Artists had previously purchased.[[55]](#cite_note-Fabrikant-56) On August 21, 1986, United Artists announced its re-entry into film production; *[Baby Boom](/wiki/Baby_Boom_(film) "Baby Boom (film)")* and *[Real Men](/wiki/Real_Men_(film) "Real Men (film)")* were the first new films to commence production, with a slate of 26 films to follow in development.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

United Artists was renamed [MGM/UA Communications Company](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer") and organized into three main units: one television production and two film units. David Gerber headed up the television unit with Anthony Thomopoulous at United Artists, and Alan Ladd, Jr. at MGM. Despite a resurgence at the box office in 1987 with *[Spaceballs](/wiki/Spaceballs "Spaceballs")*, *[The Living Daylights](/wiki/The_Living_Daylights "The Living Daylights")*, and *[Moonstruck](/wiki/Moonstruck "Moonstruck")*, MGM/UA lost $88 million.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11) That November, [Hoyts](/wiki/Hoyts "Hoyts") and United Artists decided to end their co-production partnership.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

In April 1988, Kerkorian's 82 percent of MGM/UA was up for sale; MGM and UA were split by July. Eventually, 25 percent of MGM was offered to [Burt Sugarman](/wiki/Burt_Sugarman "Burt Sugarman"), and producers [Jon Peters](/wiki/Jon_Peters "Jon Peters") and [Peter Guber](/wiki/Peter_Guber "Peter Guber"), but the plan later fell through. Rich, Ladd, Thomopoulous and other executives grew tired of Kerkorian's antics and began to leave.[[11]](#cite_note-fu-11) By summer 1988, the mass exodus of executives started to affect productions, with many film cancellations. The 1989 sale of MGM/UA to the Australian company [Qintex](/wiki/Qintex "Qintex")/[Australian Television Network](/wiki/Seven_Network "Seven Network") (owners of the Hal Roach library, which both MGM and United Artists had distributed in the 1930s) also fell through, due to the company's bankruptcy later that year; Ted Turner attempted to buy the assets again but failed.[[59]](#cite_note-60) UA was essentially dormant after 1990 and released no films for several years.

### The 1990s

In November 1990, Italian financier [Giancarlo Parretti](/wiki/Giancarlo_Parretti "Giancarlo Parretti") purchased MGM/UA. He'd previously purchased [Cannon Films](/wiki/The_Cannon_Group,_Inc. "The Cannon Group, Inc.") and renamed it Pathé Communications, anticipating a successful purchase of [Pathé](/wiki/Path%C3%A9 "Pathé"), the famed French film company. But his attempt failed – largely from the French government looking into Parretti's shady past – and instead he merged MGM/UA with his former company, resulting in [MGM-Pathé Communications](/wiki/MGM-Path%C3%A9_Communications "MGM-Pathé Communications") Co. During the transaction, Parretti overstated his own financial condition and obtained a loan under false pretenses; this was a harbinger of the chaos the studio fell into under his ownership. Debts went unpaid, forcing the delay of several films, as Parretti looted the company, fired most of the financial staff and feuded with Alan Ladd Jr. over control. To prevent any further damage (and in part to prevent their own financial misdeeds from coming to light), [Crédit Lyonnais](/wiki/Cr%C3%A9dit_Lyonnais "Crédit Lyonnais"), Parretti's primary lender, foreclosed on the studio in 1992.[[60]](#cite_note-61)[[61]](#cite_note-PBart-62)[[31]](#cite_note-Fleming-32) This resulted in Parretti defaulting on his loans and subsequently being convicted of securities fraud. On July 2, 1992, MGM-Pathé Communications was again named [Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer, Inc.](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer") In an effort to make MGM/UA saleable, Credit Lyonnais ramped up production and convinced [John Calley](/wiki/John_Calley "John Calley") to run UA. Under his supervision, Calley revived the *Pink Panther* and *James Bond* franchises and highlighted UA's past by giving the widest release ever to a film with an NC-17 rating, *[Showgirls](/wiki/Showgirls "Showgirls")*. Credit Lyonnais sold MGM in 1996, again to Kirk Kerkorian's Tracinda, leading to Calley's departure.[[31]](#cite_note-Fleming-32)

In 1999, filmmaker [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola") attempted to buy UA from Kerkorian who rejected the offer. Coppola signed a production deal with the studio instead.[[27]](#cite_note-Medavoy-28)

### The 2000s to the 2020s

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/United-Artists-Logo.svg/250px-United-Artists-Logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:United-Artists-Logo.svg)

Logo used from 2000 to 2010

In June 1999, MGM announced that it would restructure United Artists as an arthouse film producer/distributor, while the existing mainstream film production and staff of the company would be consolidated under MGM itself. Both MGM and UA commonly competed against one another in the independent film market, and the restructuring would allow UA to solely focus its attention on competing with [Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Studios_(division) "Walt Disney Studios (division)")'s [Miramax Films](/wiki/Miramax_Films "Miramax Films") division. G2 Films, the renamed [Samuel Goldwyn Company](/wiki/The_Samuel_Goldwyn_Company "The Samuel Goldwyn Company"), was renamed as United Artists International but retained the same management and staff.[[62]](#cite_note-63) The distributorship, branding, and copyrights for two of UA's main franchises (*Pink Panther*, and *Rocky*) were moved to MGM, although select MGM releases (notably the James Bond franchise co-held with [Danjaq, LLC](/wiki/Danjaq "Danjaq") and the *[Amityville Horror](/wiki/The_Amityville_Horror_(2005_film) "The Amityville Horror (2005 film)")* remake) carry a United Artists copyright. The first arthouse film to bear the UA name was *[Things You Can Tell Just by Looking at Her](/wiki/Things_You_Can_Tell_Just_by_Looking_at_Her "Things You Can Tell Just by Looking at Her")*.

In July 2000, MGM announced that it would shut down United Artists International and release all future UA films overseas under its new distribution deal with [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Studios "20th Century Studios") that was due to begin in November of that year. MGM stated that the closure had been planned as early as December 1999, stating that lucrative international TV output deals had affected the box office results for UA's recent films internationally. The mainstream North American division was unaffected by the changes.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

United Artists hired [Bingham Ray](/wiki/Bingham_Ray "Bingham Ray") to run the company on September 1, 2001.[[31]](#cite_note-Fleming-32) Under his supervision, the company produced and distributed many art films, including *[Bowling for Columbine](/wiki/Bowling_for_Columbine "Bowling for Columbine")*, 2002's *[Nicholas Nickleby](/wiki/Nicholas_Nickleby_(2002_film) "Nicholas Nickleby (2002 film)")*, and the winner of that year's [Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Foreign_Language_Film "Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film"), *[No Man's Land](/wiki/No_Man%27s_Land_(2001_film) "No Man's Land (2001 film)")*; and 2004's *[Undertow](/wiki/Undertow_(2004_film) "Undertow (2004 film)")*, and *[Hotel Rwanda](/wiki/Hotel_Rwanda "Hotel Rwanda")*, a co-production of UA and [Lions Gate Entertainment](/wiki/Lions_Gate_Entertainment "Lions Gate Entertainment"), and made deals with companies like [American Zoetrope](/wiki/American_Zoetrope "American Zoetrope") and [Revolution Films](/wiki/Revolution_Films "Revolution Films").[[64]](#cite_note-65) Ray stepped down from the company in 2004.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

In 2005, a partnership of [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast"), [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") and several merchant banks bought United Artists and its parent, MGM, for $4.8 billion.[[31]](#cite_note-Fleming-32) Though only a minority investor, Sony closed MGM's distribution system and folded most of its staff into its own studio. The movies UA had completed and planned for release—*[Capote](/wiki/Capote_(film) "Capote (film)")*, *[Art School Confidential](/wiki/Art_School_Confidential_(film) "Art School Confidential (film)")*, *[The Woods](/wiki/The_Woods_(2006_film) "The Woods (2006 film)")*, and *[Romance and Cigarettes](/wiki/Romance_and_Cigarettes "Romance and Cigarettes")* [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]—were reassigned to [Sony Pictures Classics](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Classics "Sony Pictures Classics").[[31]](#cite_note-Fleming-32)

In March 2006, MGM announced that it would return again as a domestic distribution company. Striking distribution deals with [The Weinstein Company](/wiki/The_Weinstein_Company "The Weinstein Company") (TWC), [Lakeshore Entertainment](/wiki/Lakeshore_Entertainment "Lakeshore Entertainment"), Bauer Martinez Entertainment, and other independent studios, MGM distributed films from these companies. MGM continued funding and co-producing projects released in conjunction with Sony's [Columbia TriStar Motion Picture Group](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Motion_Picture_Group "Sony Pictures Motion Picture Group") on a limited basis and produced [tent-poles](/wiki/Tent-pole_(entertainment) "Tent-pole (entertainment)") for its own distribution company, MGM Distribution.

Sony had a minority stake in MGM, but otherwise MGM and UA operated under the direction of Stephen Cooper (CEO and minority owner of MGM).

#### United Artists Entertainment

On November 2, 2006, MGM announced that [Tom Cruise](/wiki/Tom_Cruise "Tom Cruise") and his long-time production partner [Paula Wagner](/wiki/Paula_Wagner "Paula Wagner") were resurrecting UA.[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68) This announcement came after the duo were released from a fourteen-year production relationship at [Viacom](/wiki/Viacom_(2005%E2%80%93present) "Viacom (2005–present)")-owned Paramount Pictures. Cruise, Wagner and [MGM Studios](/wiki/MGM_Studios "MGM Studios") created United Artists Entertainment LLC and the producer/actor and his partner owned a 30% stake in the studio,[[68]](#cite_note-Fritz-69) with the approval by MGM's consortium of owners. The deal gave them control over production and development. Wagner was named CEO, and was allotted an annual slate of four films with varying budget ranges, while Cruise served as a producer for the revamped studio and the occasional star.

UA became the first motion picture studio granted a [Writers Guild of America, West](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America,_West "Writers Guild of America, West") (WGA) waiver in January 2008 during the [Writers' Strike](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Writers_Guild_of_America_strike "2007–08 Writers Guild of America strike").[[69]](#cite_note-70)

On August 14, 2008, MGM announced that Wagner would leave UA to produce films independently.[[70]](#cite_note-Fleming2-71) Her output as head of UA was two films, both starring Cruise, *[Lions for Lambs](/wiki/Lions_for_Lambs "Lions for Lambs")*[[71]](#cite_note-72) and *[Valkyrie](/wiki/Valkyrie_(film) "Valkyrie (film)")*.[[72]](#cite_note-bom-73) Wagner's departure led to speculation that a UA overhaul was imminent.[[70]](#cite_note-Fleming2-71)

Since then, UA has served as a co-producer with MGM for two releases: the 2009 remake of *[Fame](/wiki/Fame_(2009_film) "Fame (2009 film)")* and *[Hot Tub Time Machine](/wiki/Hot_Tub_Time_Machine "Hot Tub Time Machine")*—these are the last original films to date to bear the UA banner.

A 2011 financial report revealed that MGM reacquired its 100% stake in United Artists.[[68]](#cite_note-Fritz-69) MGM stated that it might continue to make new films under the UA brand.[[68]](#cite_note-Fritz-69) Currently, however, UA itself functions in-name-only, although it occasionally re-releases (via MGM) most of its films theatrically under the UA banner.

#### United Artists Media Group and United Artists Digital Studios

On September 22, 2014, MGM acquired a 55% interest in One Three Media and Lightworkers Media, both operated by Mark Burnett and Roma Downey and partly owned by [Hearst Entertainment](/wiki/Hearst_Corporation "Hearst Corporation"). The two companies were consolidated into a new television company, United Artists Media Group (UAMG), a revival of the UA brand. Burnett became UAMG's CEO and Downey became president of Lightworkers Media, the UAMG family and faith division. UAMG became the distributing studio for Mark Burnett Productions programming such as *[Survivor](/wiki/Survivor_(American_TV_series) "Survivor (American TV series)")*. UAMG was to form an [over-the-top](/wiki/Over-the-top_content "Over-the-top content") faith-based channel.[[31]](#cite_note-Fleming-32)[[73]](#cite_note-74)

On December 14, 2015, MGM announced that it had acquired the remaining 45% stake of UAMG it did not already own and folded UAMG into its own television division. Hearst, Downey and Burnett received stakes in MGM collectively valued at $233 million. Additionally, Burnett was promoted to president for MGM Television, replacing the outgoing [Roma Khanna](/wiki/Roma_Khanna "Roma Khanna"). The planned over-the-top faith service (later to be branded as a combined OTT/[digital subchannel](/wiki/Digital_subchannel "Digital subchannel") service known as Light TV, now the [TheGrio](/wiki/TheGrio "TheGrio")) became a separate entity owned by MGM, Burnett, Downey and Hearst.[[3]](#cite_note-MGMTV-3)

On August 14, 2018, [The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter") reported that MGM revived the brand as United Artists Digital Studios for the *[Stargate Origins](/wiki/Stargate_Origins "Stargate Origins")* web series as part of an attempted relaunch of [its *Stargate* franchise](/wiki/Stargate_(franchise) "Stargate (franchise)") which also included a dedicated [streaming media](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") platform known as *Stargate Command*, thus following in the footsteps of [Paramount Global](/wiki/Paramount_Global "Paramount Global")'s *CBS All Access* platform (now [Paramount+](/wiki/Paramount%2B "Paramount+")).[[74]](#cite_note-75)

#### 2024–present: Revival

In July 2024, it was reported that [Scott Stuber](/wiki/Scott_Stuber "Scott Stuber") was nearing a deal to revive the United Artists label under Amazon MGM Studios for the first time in over a decade. Amazon confirmed the news shortly after in a press release, announcing that Stuber had entered a multi-year first look deal with the company. Under this deal, Stuber would produce films for the newly revived banner under his own company either for a theatrical release or streaming release through [Amazon Prime Video](/wiki/Amazon_Prime_Video "Amazon Prime Video"). Stuber would also be involved with every film released under the United Artists banner.[[75]](#cite_note-revival-76)

## Film library

Main article: [List of United Artists films](/wiki/List_of_United_Artists_films "List of United Artists films")

A majority of UA's post-1952 library is now owned by MGM, while the pre-1952 films (with few exceptions) were either sold to other companies such as [National Telefilm Associates](/wiki/National_Telefilm_Associates "National Telefilm Associates") (now a part of the [Melange/Republic Pictures](/wiki/Republic_Pictures "Republic Pictures") holdings owned by Paramount Skydance, with [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") handling their distribution) or are in the [public domain](/wiki/Public_domain "Public domain"). However, throughout the studio's history, UA acted more as a distributor than a film studio, crediting the copyright to the production company responsible. This explains why certain UA releases, such as *High Noon* (1952) and *[The Final Countdown](/wiki/The_Final_Countdown_(film) "The Final Countdown (film)")* (1980), are still under copyright but not owned by MGM.[*[original research?](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")*] The MGM titles which UA distributed from 1973 to 1982 are now owned by Turner (under [Warner Bros. Pictures](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures")).

## UA films on video

UA originally leased the home video rights to its films to [Magnetic Video](/wiki/Magnetic_Video "Magnetic Video"), the first home video company. Fox purchased Magnetic in 1981 and renamed it [20th Century-Fox Video](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox_Home_Entertainment "20th Century Fox Home Entertainment") that year. In 1982, 20th Century-Fox Video merged with [CBS Video Enterprises](/wiki/CBS_Home_Entertainment "CBS Home Entertainment") (which earlier split from [MGM/CBS Home Video](/wiki/MGM_Home_Entertainment "MGM Home Entertainment") after MGM merged with UA) giving birth to [CBS/Fox Video](/wiki/CBS/Fox_Video "CBS/Fox Video"). Although MGM owned UA around this time, UA's licensing deal with CBS/Fox (which also included sublabels Key Video and Playhouse Video) was still in effect. However, the newly renamed [MGM/UA Home Video](/wiki/MGM_Home_Entertainment "MGM Home Entertainment") started releasing some UA product, including UA films originally released in the mid-1980s. Prior to MGM's purchase, UA licensed foreign video rights to Warner Bros. through [Warner Home Video](/wiki/Warner_Home_Video "Warner Home Video"), in a deal that was set to expire in 1991.[[76]](#cite_note-77) In 1986, the pre-1950 WB and the pre-May 1986 MGM film and television libraries were purchased by Ted Turner after his short-lived ownership of MGM/UA, and as a result CBS/Fox lost home video rights to the pre-1950 WB films to MGM/UA Home Video, which licensed them from Turner. When the deal with CBS/Fox (inherited from Magnetic Video) expired in 1989, the UA released films were released through MGM/UA Home Video.

Before the Magnetic Video and Warner Home Video deals in 1980, United Artists had exclusive rental contracts with a small video label called [VidAmerica](/wiki/VidAmerica "VidAmerica") in the US, and another small label called Intervision Video in the UK.[[77]](#cite_note-ncn-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80) for the home video release of 20 titles from the UA library (e.g. *[The Great Escape](/wiki/The_Great_Escape_(film) "The Great Escape (film)")*, *[Some Like It Hot](/wiki/Some_Like_It_Hot "Some Like It Hot")*, and *[Hair](/wiki/Hair_(film) "Hair (film)")*, along with a few pre-1950 WB titles).

## United Artists Broadcasting

United Artists owned and operated two television stations under the "United Artists Broadcasting" name: [WUAB](/wiki/WUAB "WUAB") in [Cleveland, Ohio](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland") (nominally licensed to [Lorain, Ohio](/wiki/Lorain,_Ohio "Lorain, Ohio")) which the studio built and sign on in 1968,[[80]](#cite_note-AkronB19680913p_43-81) [WRIK-TV](/wiki/WSTE-DT "WSTE-DT") in [San Juan, Puerto Rico](/wiki/San_Juan,_Puerto_Rico "San Juan, Puerto Rico"), which was purchased in 1969,[[81]](#cite_note-82) and held a construction permit for a station in [Houston, Texas](/wiki/Houston "Houston").[[82]](#cite_note-83) In 1970, United Artists purchased radio station [WWSH](/wiki/WUMR_(FM) "WUMR (FM)") in [Philadelphia, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia").[[83]](#cite_note-wumr-hc2-84)

United Artists left the broadcasting business starting in 1977 by selling WUAB to the [Gaylord Broadcasting Company](/wiki/Gaylord_Entertainment_Company "Gaylord Entertainment Company")[[84]](#cite_note-CPD19781010p7B-85) and WWSH to [Cox Enterprises](/wiki/Cox_Enterprises "Cox Enterprises"),[[83]](#cite_note-wumr-hc2-84) followed by WRIK-TV's sale to [Tommy Muñiz](/wiki/Tommy_Mu%C3%B1iz "Tommy Muñiz") in 1979.[[85]](#cite_note-hc-86)

## United Artists Releasing

Main article: [United Artists Releasing](/wiki/United_Artists_Releasing "United Artists Releasing")

**United Artists Releasing, LLC (UAR)** was a local [film distribution](/wiki/Film_distribution "Film distribution") [joint venture](/wiki/Joint_venture "Joint venture") between MGM and Annapurna Pictures founded by former MGM CEO [Gary Barber](/wiki/Gary_Barber "Gary Barber") and Annapurna founder [Megan Ellison](/wiki/Megan_Ellison "Megan Ellison") on 31 October 2017,[[4]](#cite_note-MFound-4) it rebranded as *United Artists Releasing* on 5 February 2019 to commemorate 100 years since the founding of *United Artists*,[[5]](#cite_note-Rebrand-5) it operated within with offices of the headquarters of the respective companies in [West Hollywood](/wiki/West_Hollywood "West Hollywood") and [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") in [California](/wiki/California "California") and offered alternative services to the [major film studios](/wiki/Major_film_studios "Major film studios") and [streaming media](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") companies[[5]](#cite_note-Rebrand-5) with 10–14 films released annually.[[6]](#cite_note-Rebrand1-6)

On 26 May 2021, [online shopping](/wiki/Online_shopping "Online shopping") and [technology company](/wiki/Technology_company "Technology company") [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)") acquired MGM Holdings, the parent company of MGM, for $8.45 billion[[86]](#cite_note-87) which was completed on 17 March 2022[[87]](#cite_note-Closed-88) and consequentially placed United Artists Releasing under the control of [Amazon Studios](/wiki/Amazon_Studios "Amazon Studios"). Amazon then folded United Artists Releasing into MGM on 4 March 2023 in a push towards cinematic/theatrical film distribution alongside their staple media releases on their video on demand service [Amazon Prime Video](/wiki/Amazon_Prime_Video "Amazon Prime Video") following the box-office success of *[Creed III](/wiki/Creed_III "Creed III")*.[[7]](#cite_note-Creed-7)
